title: Weighted Sums & the Threshold
concept: A perceptron multiplies each input by its own weight, adds all those products into one weighted sum, and fires (outputs 1) only if that sum crosses a set threshold — otherwise it stays quiet (outputs 0).

exercises.prompt: Recap: What made Rosenblatt's 1958 Perceptron different from a hard-wired logic gate?

options.text: It could learn its own weights from examples instead of a human setting fixed rules
It used a completely different kind of electricity
It could only ever output the number 0

exercises.explain: Right! The Perceptron's superpower was learning its weights from examples. Today: exactly how it uses those weights to decide when to fire.

cards.title: Deciding whether to grab an umbrella
cards.body: Imagine deciding whether to bring an umbrella. You weigh a few clues: is the sky cloudy? Did the weather app say rain? Is it windy? None of those clues matters equally — a rain forecast might sway you more than clouds alone. Add up how convinced you are, and if it passes your personal "worth it" point, you grab the umbrella. A perceptron makes decisions exactly the same way.

cards.title: What is a weighted sum?
cards.body: A weighted sum is what you get when you multiply each input by its own weight, then add all of those products together. A perceptron computes this weighted sum first — then compares it to a threshold, a cutoff number. Cross the threshold, and it fires (1). Fall short, and it stays quiet (0).

cards.title: Like a carnival game that needs enough force to ring a bell
cards.body: Picture a carnival strongman game: hit the base hard enough, and a puck rises to ring the bell at the top. Every hit adds force, but only if the total clears the bell's height does anything happen. A perceptron's threshold is that bell — the weighted sum has to clear it before the perceptron fires.

cards.title: Doing the math on one example
cards.body: Say a perceptron has two inputs: x1 = 1 and x2 = 0, with weights w1 = 2 and w2 = 3, and a threshold of 2. The weighted sum is (1 × 2) + (0 × 3) = 2. Since 2 meets the threshold of 2, the perceptron fires and outputs 1. Change x1 to 0, and the sum drops to 0 — not enough to fire.

cards.title: A bigger weight isn't automatically 'better'
cards.body: It's easy to assume a higher weight always means a 'stronger' or 'more correct' input. Really, a weight is just how much that one input counts toward the sum — it could even be small on purpose, if that input barely matters. What decides firing isn't any single weight, it's whether the whole weighted sum clears the threshold.

cards.title: Below Threshold vs. Above Threshold
cards.body: The exact same math, two different outcomes:

left.text:
Weighted sum falls short
Perceptron stays quiet
Output = 0

right.text:
Weighted sum clears the bar
Perceptron fires
Output = 1

exercises.prompt: A perceptron has one input x1 = 1 with weight w1 = 4, and a threshold of 3. What does it output?

options.text: 1, because the weighted sum (4) meets the threshold (3)
0, because the weight is too small
It can't be calculated without more inputs

exercises.explain: Right — the weighted sum is 1 × 4 = 4, which clears the threshold of 3, so the perceptron fires and outputs 1.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A perceptron adds up its weighted inputs first, then compares that sum to a threshold to decide whether to fire.
exercises.explain: True! Weighted sum first, then compare to threshold — that's the entire firing decision.

exercises.sentence: A perceptron only fires (outputs 1) if its weighted sum crosses a set ___.
exercises.explain: The threshold is the cutoff line — cross it and the perceptron fires; fall short and it stays quiet.

exercises.prompt: Which of these correctly describes how a perceptron computes its weighted sum?

options.text: Multiply each input by its matching weight, then add all the results together
Add all the inputs together, then multiply by the threshold
Pick the single largest input and ignore the rest

exercises.explain: Exactly — multiply each input by its own weight first, then sum every product. That total is compared to the threshold.

exercises.prompt: A perceptron has two inputs: x1 = 1 and x2 = 1, with weights w1 = 1 and w2 = 1, and a threshold of 2. What does it output, and why?

options.text: 1, because the weighted sum is (1×1)+(1×1) = 2, which meets the threshold of 2
0, because two separate inputs can never add up to enough
1, because any input of 1 always causes firing, regardless of weights
It cannot fire because there is more than one input

exercises.explain: Correct! (1×1) + (1×1) = 2, which meets the threshold, so it fires and outputs 1. Notice these are the same weights and threshold a perceptron would need to act just like an AND gate — next up: how a perceptron actually learns weights like these from its wrong guesses.
